CLINICAL INDICATION: 22-year-old male presenting with acute onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. No history of trauma. Evaluate for pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: There is a right apical pneumothorax with a clearly visible visceral pleural line identified at the right apex. The pneumothorax measures approximately 1.5 cm at the apex, consistent with less than 15% estimated volume loss. No contralateral pleural line is identified. The left lung demonstrates normal aeration without focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or mass lesion. No fluid level is identified on the right to suggest hemopneumothorax.

Pleura: Minimal pleural thickening is noted at the right apex, raising the possibility of an underlying apical bleb or bullous disease as the etiology of the spontaneous pneumothorax. No left-sided pleural abnormality.

Mediastinum and Heart: The cardiomediastinal silhouette is normal in width and configuration. The trachea is midline. No mediastinal shift is identified. No tension physiology.

Bones and Soft Tissues: No rib fractures are identified. No subcutaneous emphysema is present. The visualized osseous structures are otherwise intact.

Diaphragm: Both hemidiaphragms are normally positioned and intact. No diaphragmatic inversion.

IMPRESSION:

1. Small right apical pneumothorax, estimated less than 15% volume loss, without evidence of tension physiology or mediastinal shift. Imaging features suggest a primary spontaneous pneumothorax, possibly related to apical bleb rupture.
2. Mild right apical pleural thickening; underlying apical bullous disease cannot be excluded and may warrant further evaluation with CT if clinically indicated following resolution.
3. No hemothorax, rib fractures, or contralateral pulmonary abnormality.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male presenting with acute onset left-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. No antecedent trauma. History of tall, thin habitus. Rule out pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: A left-sided pneumothorax is identified. A distinct visceral pleural line is visualized in the left upper lung zone, approximately 2.1 cm from the chest wall at the apex. The estimated volume loss is less than 15%, consistent with a small apical pneumothorax. No evidence of mediastinal shift or contralateral tracheal deviation. The right lung is clear and well-expanded without focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. No air-fluid level is identified.

Pleura: No pleural thickening or pleural effusion on the left. No prior chest tube tract is identified.

Mediastinum: The trachea is midline. Mediastinal contour is within normal limits. The cardiac silhouette is normal in size and configuration. No mediastinal shift or diaphragmatic inversion is present.

Chest Wall: No rib fractures are identified. No subcutaneous emphysema is detected along the chest wall or soft tissues of the neck.

Bones: No acute osseous abnormality.

Diaphragm: Both hemidiaphragms are appropriately positioned. No diaphragmatic inversion.

IMPRESSION:

1. Small left apical pneumothorax, estimated less than 15% volume loss, with a visible visceral pleural line approximately 2.1 cm from the apex. No tension physiology. Findings are compatible with primary spontaneous pneumothorax in the appropriate clinical context.
2. No pneumothorax on the right.
3. No rib fractures, subcutaneous emphysema, or pleural effusion.
4. Recommend close clinical follow-up and repeat imaging; if symptoms worsen, aspiration or chest tube placement may be warranted.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male presenting with acute onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. History of prior spontaneous pneumothorax on the left. Evaluate for recurrent pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA and lateral chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: There is a moderate right-sided pneumothorax with a visible pleural line identified approximately 3.5 cm from the lateral chest wall at the level of the mid-lung zone, consistent with an estimated 30–35% volume loss. The right lung demonstrates partial collapse with preservation of bronchovascular markings in the medial lung. No tension physiology is identified; the mediastinum remains midline without contralateral shift. The right hemidiaphragm is depressed but not inverted.

The left lung is clear without focal consolidation, effusion, or pneumothorax. There is mild pleural thickening at the left apex, compatible with sequela of the patient's reported prior left-sided pneumothorax. No discrete bullae or blebs are identified on this examination, though underlying apical bullous disease cannot be excluded.

Pleura: No pleural effusion or hemothorax is identified on either side. No air-fluid level is present.

Bones and soft tissues: No rib fractures identified. No subcutaneous emphysema is detected in the soft tissues of the chest wall or neck. No osseous lesion is identified.

Mediastinum and hila: The cardiac silhouette is normal in size and configuration. The mediastinum is midline. Hilar contours are within normal limits. The trachea is midline.

IMPRESSION:

1. Moderate right-sided pneumothorax, estimated 30–35% volume loss, without tension physiology or mediastinal shift.
2. No hemothorax or pleural effusion.
3. Mild left apical pleural thickening, consistent with sequela of prior left-sided pneumothorax as reported clinically.
4. Clinical correlation recommended; given the moderate size and symptomatic presentation, chest tube placement or aspiration should be considered per clinical judgment.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male presenting with acute onset left-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. No antecedent trauma. History of tall, thin body habitus. Rule out pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA and lateral chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

There is a large left-sided pneumothorax with complete collapse of the left lung, which is retracted to the left hilum. A visceral pleural line is clearly visible. The degree of volume loss exceeds 60%. There is marked leftward tracheal shift and rightward deviation of the cardiac silhouette, consistent with tension physiology. The left hemidiaphragm is flattened and displaced inferiorly, with early inversion of the left hemidiaphragmatic contour. The intercostal spaces on the left are widened.

No rib fractures are identified. No subcutaneous emphysema is detected along the chest wall soft tissues bilaterally. No pleural thickening or pleural fluid is identified on the right. The right lung is hyperinflated but clear, without focal consolidation, effusion, or pneumothorax. The right hemidiaphragm is well-defined and in normal position. The mediastinum demonstrates rightward shift with displacement of the cardiac apex. No prior chest tube tracts or surgical changes are identified.

Osseous structures are otherwise unremarkable. No acute fractures detected.

IMPRESSION:

1. Large left-sided tension pneumothorax with greater than 60% volume loss, complete left lung collapse retracted to the hilum, leftward tracheal deviation, rightward mediastinal shift, and inversion of the left hemidiaphragm — findings consistent with tension physiology requiring emergent decompression.
2. No evidence of hemothorax or associated rib fractures.
3. Right lung hyperinflated but otherwise clear.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male presenting with acute onset right-sided chest pain and dyspnea. No prior history of lung disease. Evaluate for pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: There is a moderate right-sided pneumothorax with an estimated 35–40% volume loss. A well-defined visceral pleural line is identified in the right hemithorax, paralleling the lateral chest wall, with absence of lung markings peripheral to this line. The right lung demonstrates partial collapse with the lung edge visible at approximately the level of the mid-clavicular line. No contralateral pneumothorax is identified. The left lung is clear without focal consolidation, effusion, or mass.

Pleura: No pleural thickening identified. No fluid level is seen in the right hemithorax to suggest hemopneumothorax. No prior chest tube tracts noted.

Mediastinum: The mediastinum is midline without shift. The trachea is midline. No tension physiology identified. Cardiac silhouette is normal in size and contour.

Bony thorax and soft tissues: No rib fractures identified. No subcutaneous emphysema in the chest wall or neck soft tissues. No osseous destructive lesions.

Diaphragm: Right hemidiaphragm is normal in position and configuration without evidence of inversion. Left hemidiaphragm is unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:

1. Moderate right-sided pneumothorax with approximately 35–40% estimated volume loss. No tension physiology or mediastinal shift.
2. No hemothorax or associated pleural effusion.
3. No rib fractures or subcutaneous emphysema.
4. Left lung clear. No additional acute cardiopulmonary abnormality.

Clinical correlation is recommended. Given the degree of volume loss, clinical assessment for chest tube placement is advised.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male with sudden onset left-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. No history of trauma. Known tall, thin habitus. Evaluate for pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA and lateral chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

**Pleural/Pneumothorax:** There is a moderate left-sided pneumothorax with a clearly visible visceral pleural line identified in the left hemithorax. The estimated volume loss is approximately 30–35%. No mediastinal shift is identified. The left hemidiaphragm remains in normal position without evidence of inversion.

**Pleura/Soft Tissues:** Trace left apical pleural thickening is present, raising the possibility of a prior subclinical pneumothorax or bleb-associated pleural reaction. No subcutaneous emphysema is identified in the chest wall or neck soft tissues.

**Osseous Structures:** No acute rib fractures. No sternal abnormality. The visualized thoracic spine is unremarkable.

**Mediastinum:** The trachea is midline. Cardiac silhouette is normal in size and configuration. The mediastinum is not widened and shows no shift.

**Lungs:** The partially collapsed left lung demonstrates no focal consolidation or mass in its compressed parenchyma. The right lung is clear without focal opacity, pleural effusion, or pleural line. No hilar adenopathy bilaterally.

**Diaphragm:** Right hemidiaphragm is well-defined and at normal level. Left hemidiaphragm is mildly effaced by the pneumothorax but is not inverted.

**Other:** No prior chest tube tracts or surgical clips identified. No air-fluid level to suggest hemopneumothorax.

IMPRESSION:

1. Moderate left-sided pneumothorax, estimated 30–35% volume loss, without tension physiology, mediastinal shift, or diaphragmatic inversion. Findings most consistent with primary spontaneous pneumothorax in this clinical context.
2. Trace left apical pleural thickening, possibly reflecting prior subclinical event or underlying apical bleb disease. Correlation with CT chest is recommended following treatment for further characterization and to evaluate for underlying bullous disease given presentation consistent with primary spontaneous pneumothorax.
3. No hemopneumothorax. No rib fractures. Contralateral lung clear.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male presenting to the emergency department following high-speed motor vehicle collision with left-sided chest wall pain and decreased breath sounds on the left. Evaluate for traumatic injury.

TECHNIQUE: Portable anteroposterior chest radiograph obtained in the supine position.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: A large left-sided pneumothorax is identified, with the visceral pleural line visible approximately 5.5 cm from the left lateral chest wall at the mid-lung level. Estimated volume loss exceeds 60%. The left lung demonstrates marked collapse with the lung parenchyma retracted toward the left hilum. A horizontal air-fluid level is identified in the left hemithorax consistent with a hemopneumothorax. No discrete pneumothorax is identified on the right; the right lung demonstrates mild basilar atelectasis but is otherwise clear.

Pleura: Left-sided hemopneumothorax as described. No pleural thickening identified on the right.

Mediastinum and heart: The mediastinum is displaced to the right, with the trachea deviated rightward from midline. The cardiac silhouette is shifted to the right. These findings are consistent with tension physiology. The right heart border remains partially visible.

Osseous structures: Acute displaced fractures of the left 4th, 5th, and 6th ribs at the posterolateral aspect are identified. No fractures are identified on the right. Soft tissue emphysema is present along the left lateral chest wall, extending into the left axillary soft tissues.

Diaphragm: The left hemidiaphragm is flattened and depressed, providing additional evidence of tension physiology. The right hemidiaphragm is elevated, likely a positional/reactive finding.

Tubes and lines: No chest tube is present. No other support lines or devices identified.

IMPRESSION:

1. Large left-sided tension pneumothorax with estimated greater than 60% volume loss, rightward mediastinal and tracheal shift, and left hemidiaphragmatic depression consistent with tension physiology. Urgent chest tube decompression is indicated.
2. Left-sided hemopneumothorax with air-fluid level, in the setting of traumatic rib fractures, consistent with concurrent hemothorax.
3. Acute displaced fractures of the left 4th, 5th, and 6th ribs at the posterolateral aspect.
4. Left lateral chest wall and axillary subcutaneous emphysema.
5. Right basilar atelectasis; otherwise unremarkable right lung.

Results communicated directly to the clinical team by telephone at the time of interpretation given urgency of findings.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male with sudden onset right-sided chest pain and dyspnea. No history of trauma. Known tall, thin habitus.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA and lateral chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: There is a moderate right-sided pneumothorax with a visible visceral pleural line identified approximately 3.5 cm from the right lateral chest wall at the level of the mid-thorax, consistent with estimated 30–35% volume loss. No tension physiology is identified. The right hemidiaphragm maintains its normal contour without inversion. The right lung demonstrates partial collapse with increased opacity in the right lower lobe consistent with compressive atelectasis. No discrete pleural effusion or hemothorax is identified on the right. The left lung is clear without focal consolidation, effusion, or pneumothorax. There is mild background bilateral apical pleural thickening, more pronounced on the right, suggesting prior subclinical episodes.

Pleura: No fluid level identified. No subcutaneous emphysema. No evidence of prior chest tube tract.

Bony thorax: No acute rib fractures or osseous abnormalities identified. No sternal abnormality.

Mediastinum: The mediastinum is midline without shift. The cardiac silhouette is normal in size and contour. The trachea is midline.

Soft tissues: No subcutaneous emphysema detected in the chest wall or neck soft tissues.

IMPRESSION:

1. Moderate right-sided pneumothorax, estimated 30–35% volume loss, without tension physiology or mediastinal shift. Findings are most consistent with primary spontaneous pneumothorax in the appropriate clinical setting.
2. Mild bilateral apical pleural thickening, right greater than left, raising the possibility of prior subclinical spontaneous pneumothorax on the right.
3. Compressive right lower lobe atelectasis secondary to the pneumothorax.
4. No hemothorax, rib fractures, or contralateral pulmonary findings.

Clinical correlation is recommended. Chest tube placement or aspiration should be considered given the moderate size and clinical symptomatology. Follow-up imaging post-intervention is advised.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male with sudden onset right-sided chest pain and dyspnea. No known trauma. History of Marfan syndrome.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA and lateral chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: There is a large right pneumothorax with a visceral pleural line identified approximately 5.5 cm from the lateral chest wall at the level of the mid-lung. Estimated volume loss exceeds 65%. The right lung is markedly collapsed and retracted toward the right hilum. There is rightward bowing of the minor fissure. Importantly, there is significant leftward shift of the mediastinum, with the trachea displaced approximately 1.5 cm to the left of midline at the level of the carina. The cardiac silhouette is displaced leftward. The right hemidiaphragm appears flattened and demonstrates early inversion, consistent with tension physiology.

No discrete pneumothorax is identified on the left. The left lung demonstrates mild basilar atelectasis without focal consolidation. There is mild bilateral hyperinflation consistent with underlying pulmonary changes in the setting of connective tissue disease.

Pleura: No pleural effusion identified on the right. No fluid level to suggest hemopneumothorax. No pleural thickening.

Soft Tissues and Osseous Structures: No rib fractures identified. No subcutaneous emphysema. The thoracic aorta appears mildly elongated and tortuous, compatible with known Marfan syndrome. No acute osseous abnormality.

Cardiac and Mediastinal Contour: Cardiomegaly is not present on the left-displaced cardiac silhouette; however, accurate assessment is limited given mediastinal shift. Superior mediastinal contours are maintained without pneumomediastinum.

IMPRESSION:

1. Large right tension pneumothorax with estimated greater than 65% volume loss, leftward mediastinal shift measuring approximately 1.5 cm at the carina, rightward tracheal deviation corrected to leftward displacement, and early right hemidiaphragmatic inversion, consistent with tension physiology. Urgent decompression is indicated.
2. Findings are consistent with spontaneous pneumothorax in the setting of known Marfan syndrome.
3. Mild left basilar atelectasis.
4. No hemothorax or rib fractures identified.
5. Mildly elongated thoracic aorta, consistent with known connective tissue disorder.

These findings were communicated directly to the clinical team at the time of interpretation.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male presenting to the emergency department following high-speed motor vehicle collision with chest wall tenderness and decreased breath sounds on the left. Evaluate for traumatic injury.

TECHNIQUE: Portable AP chest radiograph obtained in the supine position.

FINDINGS:

A large left-sided pneumothorax is identified, with complete collapse of the left lung toward the left hilum. The visceral pleural line is visible medially, with no lung markings evident in the lateral and apical left hemithorax. The volume of the left pneumothorax is estimated at greater than 60% with associated tension physiology. There is rightward mediastinal shift, with the trachea deviated to the right of midline. The cardiac silhouette is displaced rightward. The left hemidiaphragm is depressed and flattened, consistent with diaphragmatic inversion.

A curvilinear soft tissue lucency extending along the left lateral chest wall and into the left supraclavicular soft tissues is consistent with subcutaneous emphysema.

Acute fractures are identified involving the left 5th, 6th, and 7th ribs at the posterolateral aspects. A discrete air-fluid level is noted at the left costophrenic angle, compatible with a hemopneumothorax in the setting of the identified rib fractures.

The right lung is hyperinflated but clear without focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. No right-sided rib fractures are identified. The right hemidiaphragm is elevated secondary to mediastinal shift.

Osseous structures on the right are intact. No pneumomediastinum is identified.

IMPRESSION:

1. Large left-sided tension pneumothorax with greater than 60% volume loss, rightward mediastinal shift, tracheal deviation, and left diaphragmatic inversion — findings are consistent with tension physiology requiring urgent intervention.
2. Left hemopneumothorax, evidenced by air-fluid level at the left costophrenic angle, in the setting of acute left posterolateral rib fractures of ribs 5 through 7.
3. Extensive left lateral chest wall and supraclavicular subcutaneous emphysema.
4. Compensatory hyperinflation of the right lung. No contralateral pneumothorax or effusion.

These findings were communicated directly to the clinical team at the time of interpretation.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male presenting with acute onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. History of prior spontaneous pneumothorax on the left. Evaluate for recurrent pneumothorax.

There is mild pleural thickening along the left lateral pleural surface, consistent with sequela of prior left-sided pneumothorax and/or prior pleurodesis. No discrete pleural-based fluid level is identified to suggest hemopneumothorax on either side.

Pleura: No left-sided pleural effusion. No air-fluid level on the right to suggest hemopneumothorax.

Mediastinum: The mediastinum is midline without shift. The trachea is midline. Cardiac silhouette is normal in size with a cardiothoracic ratio of approximately 0.42. No significant depression of the right hemidiaphragm.

Osseous structures and soft tissues: No acute rib fractures identified. No subcutaneous emphysema. Mild pleural thickening left base as above.

Tubes and lines: No indwelling support devices or chest tubes.

IMPRESSION:

1. Moderate right-sided pneumothorax with approximately 25–30% estimated volume loss. No tension physiology. No mediastinal shift.
2. Residual left-sided pleural thickening consistent with sequela of prior left pneumothorax, possibly with prior intervention.
3. No hemopneumothorax. No rib fractures. No subcutaneous emphysema.
4. Clinical correlation recommended; these findings were communicated to the referring clinical team at the time of interpretation.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male with acute onset left-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. No known trauma. History of prior spontaneous pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: There is a moderate left-sided pneumothorax with a visible pleural line identified approximately 3.2 cm from the left lateral chest wall at the level of the mid-lung, representing estimated 35–40% volume loss of the left hemithorax. The left lung is partially collapsed with atelectatic change seen at the left base. No tension physiology is identified; the mediastinum is midline without significant shift. The right lung is clear without focal consolidation, effusion, or pneumothorax. There is mild hyperinflation of the right lung, likely compensatory.

Pleural spaces: No pleural fluid level or hemopneumothorax on the left. No right-sided effusion.

Mediastinum and hila: The cardiac silhouette is normal in size and contour. The mediastinum is central. The trachea is midline. Hilar structures are unremarkable.

Bones and soft tissues: No rib fractures identified. No subcutaneous emphysema is detected in the visualized soft tissues. Mild apical pleural thickening is noted bilaterally, consistent with prior pleural disease and the patient's reported history of previous spontaneous pneumothorax. A subtle left apical pleural bleb is suspected but not definitively characterized on this projection.

Diaphragm: Bilateral hemidiaphragms are normally positioned without inversion.

IMPRESSION:

1. Moderate left-sided spontaneous pneumothorax with approximately 35–40% volume loss. No tension physiology or mediastinal shift.
2. Bilateral apical pleural thickening consistent with prior pleural disease, concordant with history of previous spontaneous pneumothorax. Suspected left apical bleb, which may represent the site of origin; CT of the chest may be considered for further evaluation following treatment.
3. No hemopneumothorax. No rib fractures. No contralateral pneumothorax.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male presenting to the emergency department following a motor vehicle collision with chest wall pain and decreased breath sounds on the right. Evaluate for traumatic injury.

TECHNIQUE: Portable AP chest radiograph obtained in the supine position.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: There is a large right-sided pneumothorax with near-complete collapse of the right lung, which is retracted toward the right hilum, representing greater than 60% volume loss. The right hemidiaphragm is depressed and flattened, consistent with tension physiology. There is marked leftward mediastinal shift, with the trachea deviated to the left of midline and the cardiac silhouette displaced into the left hemithorax. No definite pleural fluid level is identified on this supine projection; however, a hemopneumothorax cannot be excluded. The left lung demonstrates no focal consolidation, pneumothorax, or pulmonary edema. Mild atelectasis is present at the left base.

Pleura: No left-sided pleural effusion identified.

Chest wall and osseous structures: There are acute fractures of the right 4th, 5th, and 6th ribs at the posterior axillary line without significant displacement. Subcutaneous emphysema is present within the soft tissues of the right lateral chest wall and extends into the right axillary region.

Mediastinum: Markedly displaced to the left as described. The aortic knob contour is partially obscured by the mediastinal shift. No pneumomediastinum identified.

Cardiac: The cardiac silhouette is displaced leftward; cardiac size is difficult to accurately assess in this setting.

Diaphragm: Right hemidiaphragm is depressed and partially inverted, consistent with tension physiology. Left hemidiaphragm appears intact.

Other: No gastric dilatation or subdiaphragmatic free air identified.

IMPRESSION:
1. Large right-sided tension pneumothorax with greater than 60% volume loss, right hemidiaphragm depression and partial inversion, and marked leftward mediastinal and tracheal shift, consistent with tension physiology. This is a radiologic emergency requiring immediate decompression.
2. Acute fractures of the right 4th, 5th, and 6th ribs at the posterior axillary line, consistent with the mechanism of injury.
3. Subcutaneous emphysema of the right lateral chest wall and axillary soft tissues.
4. Hemopneumothorax cannot be excluded on this supine projection; upright or decubitus imaging recommended following stabilization to assess for pleural fluid.
5. Mild left basilar atelectasis.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male presenting with acute onset left-sided chest pain and dyspnea. History of prior spontaneous pneumothorax on the right approximately three years ago.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA and lateral chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

Left lung: There is a large left-sided pneumothorax with near-complete collapse of the left lung. The visceral pleural line is clearly delineated, with the collapsed lung parenchyma retracted toward the left hilum. Estimated volume loss exceeds 60%. There is marked leftward tracheal deviation, consistent with tension physiology. The left hemidiaphragm demonstrates inversion with inferior displacement of the dome, in keeping with elevated intrathoracic pressure. No definitive air-fluid level is identified within the left pleural space.

Right lung: The right lung is hyperexpanded, consistent with compensatory overinflation. Lung markings are present throughout without focal airspace consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. Mild bilateral apical pleural thickening is noted, likely representing sequelae of prior spontaneous pneumothoraces.

Mediastinum: The mediastinum is shifted rightward with rightward displacement of the cardiac silhouette and trachea, raising concern for tension pneumothorax. The superior mediastinum is mildly widened, attributable to vascular engorgement.

Chest wall: No rib fractures are identified. No subcutaneous emphysema is seen along the chest wall or neck.

Bones and soft tissues: No acute osseous abnormality. Soft tissues are otherwise unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:

1. Large left-sided pneumothorax with estimated greater than 60% volume loss, rightward mediastinal shift, tracheal deviation, and left hemidiaphragmatic inversion, consistent with tension pneumothorax. Urgent clinical correlation and immediate decompression are strongly recommended.
2. Bilateral apical pleural thickening, likely representing sequelae of prior spontaneous pneumothoraces.
3. Right lung hyperexpansion, compensatory in nature.
4. No hemothorax or air-fluid level identified.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male presenting with acute onset left-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. No history of trauma. Known tall, thin body habitus.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA and lateral chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: There is a large left-sided pneumothorax with complete collapse of the left lung toward the left hilum. The visceral pleural line is clearly visible, with an estimated >60% volume loss of the left hemithorax. There is marked leftward tracheal deviation and mediastinal shift to the left. The left hemidiaphragm is flattened and depressed, consistent with tension physiology. No visceral pleural line is identified on the right; the right lung appears normally inflated without consolidation, effusion, or pneumothorax. No bullous or blebs are identified on the right to suggest underlying emphysematous disease, though the collapsed left lung limits evaluation of that side.

Pleura: No pleural effusion or hemothorax is identified. No pleural thickening is appreciated on the right.

Chest wall and bony structures: No rib fractures are identified. No subcutaneous emphysema is seen along the lateral chest wall or soft tissues of the neck.

Mediastinum and hila: The mediastinum is shifted leftward with tracheal deviation to the left of midline, consistent with tension physiology. The cardiac silhouette is displaced leftward. The right hilum appears unremarkable.

Diaphragm: The left hemidiaphragm is depressed and flattened. The right hemidiaphragm is at a normal level.

Soft tissues and upper abdomen: No pneumoperitoneum. No significant soft tissue abnormality.

IMPRESSION:

1. Large left-sided tension pneumothorax with estimated greater than 60% volume loss, marked leftward mediastinal shift, tracheal deviation to the left, and depression and flattening of the left hemidiaphragm, consistent with tension physiology. Urgent clinical correlation and emergent decompression are recommended.
2. No rib fractures or subcutaneous emphysema to suggest traumatic etiology; findings are consistent with spontaneous tension pneumothorax, likely primary given patient demographics.
3. Right lung is clear without evidence of contralateral pneumothorax, consolidation, or effusion.

CLINICAL INDICATION:
64-year-old male with a history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease presenting with acute onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain and progressive dyspnea. Rule out pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE:
Upright PA and lateral chest radiographs obtained.

FINDINGS:
There is a moderate right-sided pneumothorax with a visible visceral pleural line identified along the right lateral chest wall and apex, with an estimated 35–40% volume loss of the right hemithorax. No mediastinal shift is identified. The trachea remains midline. No diaphragmatic inversion is present.

The right lung demonstrates moderate underlying hyperinflation consistent with the known history of COPD, with flattening of the bilateral hemidiaphragms and an increased AP diameter of the chest. Coarsened bronchovascular markings are noted bilaterally. There is no associated pleural fluid level to suggest hemopneumothorax.

The left lung is hyperinflated but otherwise clear without focal consolidation, effusion, or pneumothorax. No subcutaneous emphysema is identified within the soft tissues of the chest wall or neck. No rib fractures are identified on this examination. No prior chest tube tracts or pleural thickening is appreciated. The cardiomediastinal silhouette is within normal limits for patient age and body habitus. Osseous structures demonstrate mild thoracic kyphosis without acute fracture.

IMPRESSION:
1. Moderate right-sided pneumothorax with approximately 35–40% volume loss. No tension physiology. Clinical correlation and chest tube or aspiration management is recommended.
2. Bilateral pulmonary hyperinflation consistent with the provided history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
3. No hemothorax or pleural effusion identified.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male presenting to the emergency department following high-speed motor vehicle collision with chest wall impact. Evaluate for traumatic injury.

TECHNIQUE: Portable AP radiograph of the chest obtained in the supine position.
